does: This paper introduces CTI4AI, a prototype system designed to systematically identify and share threat intelligence specific to AI and ML systems. The system focuses on red teaming AI models to uncover vulnerabilities and characterize properties that improve robustness. The core contribution is establishing a structured framework for documenting adversarial attack findings and security weaknesses in AI systems, then distributing this intelligence across stakeholders including model developers, users, and security professionals. The work addresses the gap between discovering vulnerabilities through red teaming and effectively communicating those findings in a standardized, actionable format that different organizations can operationalize.
connects: This work builds on established red teaming practices from cybersecurity and adversarial ML research, extending threat intelligence sharing methodologies to the AI domain. It responds to growing recognition that adversarial robustness requires both systematic vulnerability discovery and collaborative defense mechanisms. The paper connects to prior work on adversarial attacks and defenses in deep learning, as well as established vulnerability reporting frameworks like CVE databases from traditional cybersecurity. It addresses a critical gap identified in recent AI safety research showing that isolated security testing is insufficient without mechanisms to disseminate findings, drawing on lessons from information sharing initiatives like ISAC structures used in critical infrastructure protection.
enables: This work enables future standardized reporting and disclosure processes for AI security incidents, creating infrastructure for a shared vulnerability ecosystem similar to traditional software security. By establishing protocols for threat intelligence exchange, it supports the development of AI-specific security advisories and rapid defense dissemination across the ML community. The system paves the way for automated vulnerability tracking, historical vulnerability databases for AI systems, and coordinated patching strategies. It facilitates better-informed model development practices and supports regulatory compliance efforts in AI governance by providing documented evidence of security assessment and remediation processes, potentially enabling formal certification standards for AI robustness and security maturity.
